questions.question_text: A Vermont tenant signs a 10-month lease at $1,200 per month. What is the maximum security deposit the landlord may collect?
questions.explanation: For leases of one year or less, Vermont limits the security deposit to one month's rent. For leases longer than one year, the limit increases to two months' rent.

questions.question_text: A Vermont tenant with an 18-month lease at $900 per month is asked for a $2,000 security deposit. Is this lawful?
distractors: Yes, it is within the limit
questions.explanation: For leases longer than one year, Vermont allows a maximum security deposit of two months' rent. Two months at $900 equals $1,800, so $2,000 exceeds the legal limit.

questions.question_text: Within how many days must a Vermont landlord return a security deposit after the tenant vacates?
questions.explanation: Vermont landlords must return the security deposit with an itemized statement of any deductions within 14 days. Failure to do so means the landlord forfeits the right to withhold any portion.

questions.question_text: A Vermont landlord willfully fails to return a tenant's $1,000 security deposit within the required timeframe. What penalty may the court impose?
questions.correct_answer: Double the amount plus attorney fees
distractors: Return of only the deposit amount
Triple damages plus all court costs
A $500 civil fine per day of delay
questions.explanation: Willful failure to return a security deposit within 14 days in Vermont can result in the landlord paying double the deposit amount plus the tenant's attorney fees.

questions.question_text: A Vermont landlord deducts $300 from a security deposit for normal carpet wear after 3 years of tenancy. Is this deduction lawful?
questions.correct_answer: No, normal wear and tear cannot be deducted
distractors: Yes, carpet costs are always deductible
Yes, if properly documented with photographs
Yes, for any tenancy exceeding 2 years
questions.explanation: Vermont landlords may only retain security deposit funds for damage beyond normal wear and tear, unpaid utilities, or abandoned property removal. Ordinary carpet wear after 3 years is normal wear and tear.

questions.question_text: A Vermont tenant fails to pay rent. How many days does the landlord's pay-or-quit notice give the tenant?
questions.explanation: For nonpayment of rent in Vermont, the landlord must provide a 14-day notice to pay or quit. This gives the tenant two weeks to either pay the overdue rent or vacate the premises.

questions.question_text: A Vermont tenant violates a lease provision by keeping an unauthorized pet. What notice period must the landlord provide?
questions.explanation: For lease violations other than nonpayment, Vermont requires a 30-day notice to cure or quit. The tenant has 30 days to correct the violation or vacate the property.

questions.question_text: A Vermont month-to-month tenant has lived in the unit for 3 years. How much notice must the landlord give to terminate the tenancy without cause?
questions.explanation: Vermont requires 60 days' notice to terminate a month-to-month tenancy when the tenant has resided there for 2 or more years. Tenancies under 2 years require only 30 days' notice.

questions.question_text: A Vermont tenant engages in serious criminal activity on the premises. What is the minimum notice period for eviction?
questions.explanation: For criminal or serious violations in Vermont, the landlord must provide a 14-day notice to vacate. Unlike some states, Vermont does not allow shorter notice periods even for criminal activity.

questions.question_text: A Vermont month-to-month tenant has rented for 18 months. The landlord wants to end the tenancy without cause. What notice is required?
questions.explanation: For month-to-month tenancies of less than 2 years, Vermont requires 30 days' notice to terminate. The longer 60-day notice only applies when the tenant has resided for 2 or more years.

questions.question_text: A Vermont apartment has a broken furnace in January. The landlord ignores repair requests for two weeks. What remedy may the tenant pursue?
questions.explanation: Vermont tenants may use the repair-and-deduct remedy when landlords fail to maintain habitable conditions. Rent withholding is also an option. Both remedies address the landlord's duty to maintain habitability.

questions.question_text: A Vermont tenant reports a building code violation to the health department. The landlord then serves a termination notice. What legal protection applies?
distractors: The landlord may proceed if cause exists
The tenant must prove malice in court
No protection exists for this scenario
questions.explanation: Vermont prohibits retaliatory eviction. A landlord cannot terminate a tenancy in retaliation for a tenant exercising legal rights such as reporting code violations or requesting repairs.

questions.question_text: For what minimum tenancy duration does Vermont require a written lease agreement?
questions.explanation: Vermont requires written leases for tenancies of one year or longer. Shorter tenancies may be oral, though written agreements are always recommended for clarity.

questions.question_text: Which of the following must be included in a Vermont written lease agreement?
questions.correct_answer: Names, address, rent, and due date
distractors: Only the monthly rent amount and term
Only the property address and unit number
Only the lease start and end dates
questions.explanation: Vermont written leases must include the names of parties, property address, rent amount, rent due date, and statutory disclosures. These requirements ensure both parties understand the basic terms.

questions.question_text: A Vermont landlord's rental unit has persistent mold from a leaking roof. The tenant withholds rent until repairs are made. Is this lawful?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, rent withholding is a valid remedy
distractors: No, tenants must always pay rent
Yes, but only after 90 days' notice
questions.explanation: Vermont tenants may withhold rent when the landlord fails to maintain habitable conditions. Persistent mold from a structural defect violates the implied warranty of habitability.
